binding_constraint_top1: Insufficient planning coverage and outcome visibility for people leaving prison — especially remand prisoners — which prevents the system from reliably identifying need, delivering support early enough, and learning what works.
binding_constraint_top3: Weak pre-release housing planning coverage for remand populations
Lack of operational data on housing need, reintegration plans, and release outcomes
Case-management capacity and process design that underserves people on remand
slow_variables: Case-management capacity and process coverage before release
Data systems for measuring housing need, reintegration plans, and outcomes
Relationships and coordination with support organisations and housing providers
Underlying housing-market scarcity and landlord reluctance
contributing_factors: People on remand receive less planning support than sentenced prisoners
Many on remand have limited time with a case manager or do not see one at all
The department does not know how many people leave prison with a housing need or a reintegration plan
The review estimates that more than half of people leaving prison have an unmet housing need
good_interventions_ranked: Expand and redesign pre-release planning coverage for remand populations so support starts earlier and more consistently.
Build operational visibility: track housing need, reintegration plans, support quality, and release outcomes as core management metrics.
Strengthen structured coordination with support organisations and housing pathways, but tie it to measurable case-level follow-through.
bad_interventions: Treating the issue only as a broad national housing shortage with no corrections-process redesign
Launching new slogans or high-level reintegration strategies without better coverage and data
Focusing only on sentenced prisoners while remand populations continue to grow
early_warning_signals: Share of remand prisoners who receive housing planning support before release
Proportion of releases with documented housing status and reintegration plan
Rate of post-release unstable housing among people the department supported
Growing gap in planning support between remand and sentenced populations
distractors_to_penalize: Generic calls for more housing funding without corrections-process change
One-off pilots that do not close measurement gaps
evidence: The Auditor-General says people on remand receive less planning support than people serving sentences.
Many people on remand have limited time with a case manager or do not see one at all.
The department does not know how many people leave prison with a housing need or a reintegration plan.
The review says more than half of people leaving prison are estimated to have an unmet housing need.
